title: Chronic Kidney Disease
concise_summary: Progressive, irreversible loss of kidney function common in older cats.
full_text: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is a slow, progressive loss of renal function in cats. Affected cats may drink and urinate excessively, and as the disease advances they often become lethargic, lose weight, and develop vomiting and loss of appetite.

title: Otitis Externa
concise_summary: Inflammation or infection of the external ear canal.
full_text: Otitis externa is inflammation of the external ear canal in dogs, often caused by bacteria, yeast, parasites, or allergies. Dogs with otitis externa shake their heads and scratch at their ears; the ear canal becomes red, swollen, and malodorous, often with dark or yellow discharge.

title: Atopic Dermatitis
concise_summary: Chronic allergic skin disease causing intense itching (pruritus).
full_text: Atopic dermatitis is a hereditary, IgE-mediated allergic skin disease in dogs. Affected dogs suffer from chronic pruritus; they scratch, lick, and chew their skin and feet, leading to redness, hair loss, and secondary infections of skin and ears.

title: Heartworm Disease
concise_summary: Mosquito-transmitted parasitic infection affecting the heart and lungs.
full_text: Heartworm disease is caused by the parasite Dirofilaria immitis transmitted by mosquitoes. Adult worms live in the heart and pulmonary arteries, causing inflammation and damage. Common signs include coughing, reduced exercise tolerance, weight loss, and difficulty breathing.

title: Diabetes Mellitus
concise_summary: Chronic endocrine disorder causing high blood sugar due to insufficient insulin.
full_text: Diabetes mellitus is a disorder of glucose regulation due to insulin deficiency. Pets with diabetes typically show increased thirst, increased urination, increased appetite, and weight loss. It requires lifelong management (usually insulin therapy).

title: Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease
concise_summary: Group of bladder and urethral conditions causing painful, frequent urination in cats.
full_text: Feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD) refers to several conditions affecting a cat’s bladder or urethra. Affected cats often strain or cry when urinating, may only pass small amounts of urine, or urinate outside the litter box. Blood in the urine is common, and male cats may suffer life-threatening urethral obstruction.

title: Canine Parvovirus
concise_summary: Highly contagious viral gastroenteritis affecting dogs (often fatal in puppies).
full_text: Canine parvovirus is a highly contagious virus that causes severe gastroenteritis in dogs. Infected dogs typically develop acute onset of lethargy, loss of appetite, fever, and severe vomiting and often bloody diarrhea. Rapid dehydration and sepsis can occur if not treated aggressively.

title: Lyme Disease
concise_summary: Tick-transmitted bacterial infection causing fever and shifting leg lameness in dogs.
full_text: Lyme disease is caused by the bacterium *Borrelia burgdorferi*, transmitted by infected ticks. In dogs it often causes fever, lethargy, and painful swelling of joints. A hallmark sign is a sudden, shifting lameness (limping that moves from leg to leg). In rare cases it can affect the kidneys and be fatal.

title: Osteoarthritis
concise_summary: Chronic degenerative joint disease causing inflammation and pain in older dogs.
full_text: Osteoarthritis (degenerative joint disease) involves inflammation and degeneration of one or more joints. It is extremely common in older or large-breed dogs. Affected dogs often show difficulty getting up, reluctance to climb stairs or jump, and lameness that worsens with activity.

title: Gastroenteritis
concise_summary: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines causing acute vomiting and diarrhea.
full_text: Gastroenteritis refers to inflammation of the stomach and intestinal tract in dogs and cats. It typically causes sudden onset of vomiting and diarrhea, which can lead to dehydration. Affected pets often have reduced appetite and may run a fever if infection is present.
